content.text: Service Ownership Map

attrs.alt: Agentic AI microservice ownership diagram: Orchestrator Service (AI Platform Team) at the top, fanning out to Model Gateway (Platform Team), Tool Services (Feature Teams), Safety Service (Safety Team), and Eval Service (Quality/Eval Team). A dashed arrow leads down to Prompt Registry (AI Platform Team).

content.text: The diagram above shows five service categories in a typical agentic AI system and the teams responsible for each. The Orchestrator Service sits at the centre of the agent loop. Tool Services fan outward to feature-owning teams. The Safety Service has a vertical veto relationship with all other services. The Prompt Registry is a shared dependency managed by the same team that owns the orchestrator.
Why Ownership Boundaries Are Critical in Agentic Systems

A traditional web service has a well-understood blast radius: a bug in the payments service damages the payments flow. An agentic AI system is different. The orchestrator calls tool services across team boundaries at runtime. A breaking change in a tool's response schema, merged by a feature team without informing the AI Platform team, silently corrupts the agent's context assembly. The orchestrator may still accept the malformed payload, propagate it to the model, and generate plausible-looking but incorrect output. There is no type-system safety net at service boundaries.

Unclear ownership amplifies this risk. When no team is the designated owner of a service boundary, changes accumulate without review, on-call escalation has no clear target, and post-incident analysis cannot assign accountability. Ownership boundaries in agentic microservices serve three functions: they define who reviews changes to a service's interface, who is paged when that service degrades, and who approves structural modifications to the shared contract between services.
Agents call tools across service boundaries at runtime, making interface drift immediately observable in production outputs rather than at compile time.
Safety-relevant tool changes — adding a new write capability, widening an input schema — require a reviewer with authority to block the merge, not just comment on it.
Cost attribution for model calls is impossible without knowing which team owns the orchestrator routing that decides model selection and token budget.
Incident response for agentic failures requires immediate identification of whether the fault is in the orchestrator, a specific tool service, the safety filter, or the model gateway.
The Standard Ownership Model for Agentic AI
Most mature agentic AI organisations converge on three primary ownership roles with well-defined responsibilities and no overlapping authority.
AI Platform Team.

Owns the orchestrator service, the model gateway, and the prompt registry. Defines the standard interface patterns that all tool services must conform to. Sets the token budget policy and model routing rules.
Feature Teams.

Each owns one or more tool services. Responsible for implementing their tool's business logic and maintaining the tool contract (the OpenAPI or JSON Schema describing the tool's input and output). Cannot change the contract unilaterally if the change affects safety-relevant parameters.
Safety Team.
Owns the safety service and holds approval authority over any tool contract change that widens the set of actions an agent can perform. Has veto power independent of AI Platform Team sign-off.
A fourth role —
Quality / Eval Team

— owns the eval service. In smaller organisations this role is often held by a subgroup of the AI Platform Team, but at scale it warrants dedicated headcount because eval infrastructure and production orchestration have fundamentally different change cadences.
Orchestrator Service Ownership

The orchestrator service contains the agent loop: the code that sends messages to the model, interprets the model's tool-use blocks, dispatches calls to tool services, appends tool results to the message history, and decides when to stop. Because it controls what information the model sees and which tools it is offered, it is the highest-privilege component in the system.

Ownership by the AI Platform Team means that no feature team can modify the agent loop, the prompt selection logic, or the context assembly strategy without the AI Platform Team's review. This is enforced via a CODEOWNERS entry that requires a review from
@ai-platform
on any pull request touching the orchestrator path.
Agent loop logic: the
while
loop that drives plan-act-observe turns.
Prompt selection: the code that picks which system prompt variant to use based on context, user tier, or experiment flag.
Context assembly: the code that builds the messages array, trims history to fit the context window, and injects retrieved documents.
Tool registry: the mapping from tool name to tool-service endpoint, owned by AI Platform to ensure that no feature team can self-register an unsafe tool.
Tool Service Ownership

Each tool service is owned by the feature team whose product domain it serves. The search tool is owned by the Search team. The code execution tool is owned by the Developer Productivity team. The CRM write tool is owned by the Sales Automation team. This mapping is natural because those teams already carry on-call responsibility for the underlying systems their tools expose.

The boundary between feature team authority and AI Platform Team authority sits at the tool contract. A feature team has full authority over the implementation of its tool — database queries, caching strategies, retry logic. It does not have unilateral authority to change the tool's published schema, because that schema is part of the shared interface consumed by the orchestrator.
Owned by feature team:
business logic, database access patterns, API credentials, internal retry and circuit-breaker configuration.
Shared ownership (feature team + AI Platform):
the tool contract (input schema, output schema, error codes), versioning policy, SLA commitments to the orchestrator.
Requires Safety Team approval:
any schema change that adds new write operations, expands the set of resources the agent can modify, or removes a parameter that was previously used as a safety constraint.
Model Gateway Ownership

The model gateway is the single point through which all model API calls flow. It handles model routing (deciding which model serves which request class), rate limiting (enforcing per-team and per-product token quotas), cost attribution (tagging each call with the team and user session responsible), and fallback logic (switching to a smaller model when the primary is unavailable).

Centralised ownership by the AI Platform Team (sometimes called the Platform Engineering team in larger organisations) is the only practical model. If individual feature teams controlled their own model routing, enforcing organisation-wide cost caps and compliance requirements would require coordinating across every team simultaneously. A single owner means a single enforcement point for the policies that apply to all model usage.
Model routing rules: which model handles which request type, enforced centrally so that no feature team can accidentally route latency-sensitive requests to a slower model.
Rate limiting: per-team token budgets that prevent one team's experiment from exhausting the shared monthly quota.
Cost control: tagging every API call with cost centre metadata so finance teams can chargeback accurately.
Eval Service Ownership

The eval service runs automated quality checks against every significant change to the orchestrator, its prompts, or its tool contracts. Ownership by a Quality Engineering team (or a dedicated Eval Team) provides the institutional independence necessary for its results to carry weight. If the same team that builds the orchestrator also owns the eval service, there is no structural barrier preventing that team from adjusting eval thresholds to make their own changes look acceptable.
The Eval Team defines the evaluation datasets, the scoring rubrics, and the regression thresholds that block a merge.
The AI Platform Team consumes the eval results but cannot modify the passing threshold unilaterally — a threshold change requires Eval Team review.
Feature teams must provide domain-specific evaluation examples when their tool contract changes; the Eval Team integrates those examples into the regression suite.
Safety Service Ownership

The safety service sits between the orchestrator and every tool service, filtering requests before they execute and responses before they are appended to the agent's context. It applies content policies, checks that proposed tool arguments are within sanctioned bounds, and can halt an agent run when a policy violation is detected.

Safety Reviewers hold the authority to veto a tool contract change even after the AI Platform Team has approved it. This separation of concerns is intentional: a performance optimisation that happens to widen the blast radius of a tool requires sign-off from the team whose mandate is specifically to contain that blast radius.
Safety Reviewers are the required approvers in CODEOWNERS for any file that defines a tool's capability boundary.
The safety service's own policy files are owned exclusively by the Safety Team and cannot be modified by any other team.
Safety Team participates in incident retrospectives when an agent run caused an unintended action, with the authority to mandate new guardrails before the service is re-enabled.
The Tool Contract as an Ownership Boundary

A tool contract is the machine-readable description of what a tool does, what inputs it accepts, and what outputs it returns. In agentic AI systems it is typically expressed as a JSON Schema object embedded in the tools list passed to the model API. The contract is not implementation detail — it is the public interface that the orchestrator depends on and the model reasons about. Treating it as an ownership boundary means that the contract file is the primary artefact that CODEOWNERS rules protect.

Every change to a tool contract goes through a review process with at least two required approvers: one from the AI Platform Team (verifying that the change is backward-compatible with the orchestrator's expectation) and one from the Safety Team (verifying that the change does not expand the agent's capability to cause harm). Breaking changes to a tool contract require a versioning strategy — usually a new tool name such as
search_v2
— so the orchestrator and the old tool version can coexist during migration.
Code Block: Service-Level CODEOWNERS in a Monorepo

In a monorepo where all agent services live under one repository, a single CODEOWNERS file at the repository root maps directory paths to owning teams. The example below shows a realistic layout for a product called
deiva-agent

# .github/CODEOWNERS
# Applies to the deiva-agent monorepo.
# Format: <path pattern>  <owner> [<owner> ...]
# More specific patterns below override less specific ones above.

# --- Orchestrator (AI Platform Team owns everything by default) ---
/services/orchestrator/                 @deiva/ai-platform

# Prompt selection and context assembly require senior AI Platform review
/services/orchestrator/src/prompts/     @deiva/ai-platform @deiva/ai-platform-leads
/services/orchestrator/src/context/     @deiva/ai-platform @deiva/ai-platform-leads

# --- Model Gateway (Platform Engineering) ---
/services/model-gateway/                @deiva/platform-eng

# --- Tool services (each team owns its own service directory) ---
/services/tools/search/                 @deiva/search-team
/services/tools/code-exec/              @deiva/devprod-team
/services/tools/crm-write/              @deiva/sales-automation
/services/tools/document-reader/        @deiva/docs-team

# Tool CONTRACTS require both AI Platform and Safety Team sign-off
/services/tools/search/contract.json    @deiva/ai-platform @deiva/safety-reviewers
/services/tools/code-exec/contract.json @deiva/ai-platform @deiva/safety-reviewers
/services/tools/crm-write/contract.json @deiva/ai-platform @deiva/safety-reviewers
/services/tools/document-reader/contract.json @deiva/ai-platform @deiva/safety-reviewers

# --- Safety Service (Safety Team only) ---
/services/safety/                       @deiva/safety-reviewers
/services/safety/policies/              @deiva/safety-reviewers

# --- Eval Service (Eval Team) ---
/services/eval/                         @deiva/eval-team
/services/eval/thresholds.json          @deiva/eval-team

# --- Prompt Registry (AI Platform Team) ---
/prompt-registry/                       @deiva/ai-platform
/prompt-registry/production/            @deiva/ai-platform @deiva/ai-platform-leads
The key structural choice here is placing
contract.json

as a separate CODEOWNERS entry from the rest of the service directory. A feature team can merge changes to its tool's implementation with only its own review; changing the contract always triggers the cross-team review process.
Ownership in a Polyrepo Setup

Many organisations deploy each service as its own repository. In a polyrepo model, the CODEOWNERS file in each repository covers only that service's internal paths. Cross-repo ownership — defining who must approve a tool contract change that affects the orchestrator in a different repository — cannot be enforced by CODEOWNERS alone.
The standard approach is a centrally maintained
tool-registry

repository. This repository contains only tool contract files and their review history. Feature teams propose contract changes via pull requests to the tool-registry repo. CODEOWNERS in the tool-registry repo requires AI Platform Team and Safety Team approval for any contract file. The orchestrator reads its tool definitions from the tool-registry at deploy time, so the orchestrator's release automatically reflects the latest approved contracts.
Each service repo CODEOWNERS covers its internal ownership (implementation, tests, CI config).
The centralized tool-registry repo is the enforcement point for cross-repo contract changes.
Ownership metadata (team name, Slack channel, PagerDuty schedule) for each service lives in a service catalogue (e.g. Backstage) that both teams and automated tooling can query.
A dependency graph tracked in the service catalogue shows which orchestrator versions depend on which tool contract versions, enabling impact analysis before a contract is changed.
Cross-Team Review Requirements for Interface Changes

Not all interface changes carry the same risk. A well-defined escalation matrix avoids the overhead of Safety Team review for every trivial change while ensuring it is required for the changes that actually matter.
No additional review required:
narrowing an input parameter's allowed range (more restrictive), adding a new output field that the orchestrator ignores, improving an error message string.
AI Platform Team review required:
renaming a parameter, changing an output field type, adding an optional parameter that the orchestrator must learn to pass.
AI Platform Team + Safety Team review required:

adding a new write operation, removing a parameter that was previously used as a safety guard, widening an enum to include new values with side effects, increasing the maximum size of a file the agent can write.
RFC process required:
giving the agent access to an entirely new external system, adding a tool that involves financial transactions, adding a tool that accesses personal health information.
Code Block: Tool Contract Schema and Review Checklist
The following example shows a tool contract for a document-write tool and the accompanying review checklist that Safety Reviewers use when evaluating a proposed change.
Shared Infrastructure Ownership: Prompt Registry

The prompt registry is the centralised store of versioned system prompts and few-shot examples used by the orchestrator. It is shared infrastructure rather than a feature team concern, because prompt changes have system-wide effects: a prompt that instructs the agent to prefer a particular tool skews tool call patterns for all users, regardless of which feature domain they are using.

AI Platform Team ownership means that any merge to the production prompt registry requires AI Platform approval. Feature teams can propose prompt additions via pull request, but they cannot self-approve changes that affect the system prompt or the base few-shot set. Experiment-specific prompts — prompts used only in a feature-flagged A/B test — are owned by the feature team but still follow the registry's versioning conventions.
System prompts (production): owned by AI Platform Team, require AI Platform lead approval.
Task-specific instruction blocks: co-owned by feature team and AI Platform Team; require both to approve.
Safety instruction blocks: owned by Safety Team; AI Platform Team cannot merge changes without Safety Team approval.
Experiment prompts (behind feature flag): owned by the feature team running the experiment, with a time-bounded sunset clause.
Code Block: GitHub CODEOWNERS for Polyrepo Agents
In a polyrepo, the tool-registry repository's CODEOWNERS file is the primary contract-review enforcement mechanism. The following example shows a realistic layout.

# tool-registry/.github/CODEOWNERS
# This repository contains only tool contracts.
# Every contract change requires AI Platform + Safety review.
# Feature team ownership is listed for transparency but does not grant merge rights.

# Default: AI Platform Team and Safety Team must approve all changes
*                                         @deiva/ai-platform @deiva/safety-reviewers

# Per-tool contracts: feature team is listed as informational co-owner
/tools/search/                            @deiva/ai-platform @deiva/safety-reviewers @deiva/search-team
/tools/code-exec/                         @deiva/ai-platform @deiva/safety-reviewers @deiva/devprod-team
/tools/crm-write/                         @deiva/ai-platform @deiva/safety-reviewers @deiva/sales-automation
/tools/document-write/                    @deiva/ai-platform @deiva/safety-reviewers @deiva/docs-team
/tools/calendar-read/                     @deiva/ai-platform @deiva/safety-reviewers @deiva/scheduling-team

# Registry schema itself: AI Platform leads only
/registry-schema.json                     @deiva/ai-platform-leads

# Deprecation notices: AI Platform can merge without Safety (read-only change)
/deprecated/                              @deiva/ai-platform
Notice that the wildcard rule at the top provides a safe default: anything not matched by a more specific pattern requires the two mandatory approvers. The
deprecated/
exception acknowledges that removing a tool from production is lower risk than adding a capability.
Code Block: Service Registry Ownership Metadata

A service catalogue entry in Backstage (or a similar internal developer platform) captures ownership metadata that CODEOWNERS cannot express: on-call schedules, Slack channels, SLA commitments, and dependency relationships.

# backstage/catalog-info.yaml for the search tool service
apiVersion: backstage.io/v1alpha1
kind: Component
metadata:
  name: tool-search
  description: "Web search tool service consumed by the Deiva agent orchestrator."
  annotations:
    github.com/project-slug: deiva-org/tool-search
    pagerduty.com/integration-key: "<search-team-pd-key>"
    slack.com/channel: "#search-team-oncall"
  tags:
    - agentic-tool
    - search
    - production
spec:
  type: service
  lifecycle: production
  owner: group:deiva/search-team
  system: deiva-agent
  dependsOn:
    - resource:default/tool-registry
    - component:default/orchestrator
  providesApis:
    - tool-search-contract-v2
---
apiVersion: backstage.io/v1alpha1
kind: API
metadata:
  name: tool-search-contract-v2
  description: "Stable tool contract for web search. Safety-reviewed 2026-06-01."
spec:
  type: openapi
  lifecycle: production
  owner: group:deiva/search-team
  definition:
    $ref: ./contract.json
Escalation Paths: When Teams Disagree on Ownership

Ownership boundaries occasionally generate contested territory. The most common disputes are: a feature team believes a tool's safety constraint is too restrictive and blocks a legitimate product use case; the Safety Team believes a proposed contract change is unsafe but the AI Platform Team is under pressure to ship; two feature teams want ownership of a shared utility tool that neither originally built.

The RFC (Request for Comments) process provides a structured path through these disputes. A contested ownership question is written up as a short document covering: the proposed boundary, the rationale, the alternatives considered, and which teams are affected. The RFC is circulated to all affected teams with a two-week comment window. If no consensus is reached, the decision escalates to the AI Platform Team lead and the Safety Team lead jointly, whose decision is final. This avoids the escalation going all the way to a VP or CTO for routine engineering disputes.
Write the RFC as a pull request to the internal docs or architecture decision records (ADR) repository, not as a Slack thread, so the decision is discoverable and permanent.
Time-box the comment period explicitly. Unlimited comment periods produce indefinite blocking; two weeks is the standard.
Document the dissenting view even when it did not prevail. Future engineers reading the ADR need to understand why the path not taken was rejected, so they do not re-litigate settled decisions.
After the RFC resolves, update CODEOWNERS and the service catalogue to reflect the agreed boundary before closing the RFC pull request.
Ownership Handoff: Transferring a Service Between Teams

Teams reorganise. A tool service that was built and owned by the Search team may be transferred to a new Retrieval Platform team that consolidates all retrieval infrastructure. Ownership transfers that skip formal handoff steps produce gaps: the old team stops responding to incidents, the new team does not yet know the service's edge cases, and both CODEOWNERS and PagerDuty still point to the old team.
A structured handoff process prevents those gaps. The sequence has four phases: shadow on-call, transfer, validation, and cleanup.
Shadow on-call (two weeks):

the incoming team is added to the PagerDuty schedule alongside the existing team. They receive all pages but are not expected to resolve independently. The existing team answers questions and explains runbooks.
Transfer (one week):
CODEOWNERS, service catalogue, and PagerDuty are updated to list the incoming team as primary. The outgoing team is listed as secondary escalation for a defined period.
Validation (two weeks):
the incoming team handles all incidents independently. The outgoing team is available by Slack but is not on rotation.
Cleanup:
the outgoing team is removed from CODEOWNERS, PagerDuty, and the service catalogue entry. The ADR for the transfer is closed with a note recording the effective handoff date.

The same process applies to tool contract ownership. A new team that inherits a tool contract is not bound by the design decisions embedded in it, but they must propose changes through the normal review process rather than immediately rewriting the contract. This protects the orchestrator from sudden interface instability during the transition.
Key Takeaways
In agentic AI microservices, ownership boundaries are safety-relevant because the orchestrator calls tool services across team lines at runtime without type-system enforcement.

The tool contract (JSON Schema or OpenAPI spec) is the primary ownership boundary artefact. CODEOWNERS rules on contract files enforce the two-team review requirement: AI Platform Team for compatibility, Safety Team for risk.
The orchestrator, model gateway, and prompt registry are owned centrally by the AI Platform Team. Distributing these to feature teams creates uncoordinated changes to the agent's core behaviour.
The Safety Team holds veto authority over contract changes that expand agent capability, independent of other approvals. This separation of concerns is what makes the veto meaningful.
In a polyrepo setup, a centralised tool-registry repository with its own CODEOWNERS is the standard pattern for enforcing cross-repo contract review.
Ownership disputes are resolved via a time-boxed RFC process that produces a written ADR, not an informal decision that lives only in a Slack thread.
Service transfers require a structured shadow, transfer, and validation sequence before removing the outgoing team from CODEOWNERS and on-call rotations.
